title: Strings Basics
summary: Learn how to create, combine, and work with text in Python — one of the most essential skills in any program.

blocks.md: Almost every program works with text — names, messages, addresses, commands. In Python, a piece of text is called a **string**. Understanding strings is one of the very first superpowers you will use as a programmer. This lesson shows you how to create them, combine them, measure them, and avoid a few sneaky traps.

blocks.title: A String is a Necklace of Characters
blocks.md: Imagine threading letters onto a string of beads, one after another: `H`, `e`, `l`, `l`, `o`. The whole necklace is a **string**. Each bead is a single character. Python keeps them in order and remembers every one.

You create a string by wrapping text in quotes. Python accepts **single quotes** `'...'`, **double quotes** `"..."`, or **triple quotes** `'''...'''` / `"""..."""`. All three styles produce strings — choose whichever makes your code easiest to read.

blocks.caption: Single quotes and double quotes both work the same way.

blocks.md: There is no difference between single and double quotes in Python — pick one and be consistent. The real reason both exist is so you can **include one type of quote inside the other** without any trouble.

blocks.code:
# Include an apostrophe using double quotes
message = "It's a great day!"

# Include double quotes using single quotes
quote = 'She said "hello" to everyone.'

print(message)
print(quote)
blocks.output:
It's a great day!
She said "hello" to everyone.
blocks.caption: Mixing quote styles lets you include the other kind of quote inside your string.

blocks.md: Triple quotes let you write a string that spans **multiple lines** without any extra tricks. Everything between the opening `"""` and closing `"""` (or `'''`) becomes part of the string, line breaks included.

blocks.code:
poem = """
Roses are red,
Violets are blue,
Python is fun,
And so are you!
"""

print(poem)
blocks.output:
Roses are red,
Violets are blue,
Python is fun,
And so are you!
blocks.caption: Triple quotes preserve every newline exactly as you typed it.

blocks.md:
Sometimes you need to put a special character inside a string — a line break, a tab, or a literal quote — but you cannot just type it directly. Python uses a **backslash `\`** as an escape signal: it tells Python that the next character has a special meaning.

The most common escape sequences are:
- `\n` — newline (moves to the next line)
- `\t` — tab (inserts a horizontal tab)
- `\\` — a literal backslash
- `\'` — a single quote inside a single-quoted string
- `\"` — a double quote inside a double-quoted string

blocks.code:
print("Line one\nLine two")
print("Name:\tAlice")
print("C:\\Users\\Alice")
print('She said \'hi\'.')
blocks.output:
Line one
Line two
Name:	Alice
C:\Users\Alice
She said 'hi'.
blocks.caption: Escape sequences let you embed special characters inside any string.

blocks.title: Windows File Paths and Backslashes
blocks.md: On Windows, folder paths use backslashes: `C:\Users\Alice`. If you type this directly in a string, Python will misread `\U` and `\A` as escape sequences. Always **double the backslashes** (`C:\\Users\\Alice`) or use a raw string: `r'C:\Users\Alice'`.
You can glue strings together with the **`+` operator** — this is called **concatenation**. You can also repeat a string a fixed number of times using the **`*` operator**.

blocks.code:
first = "Good"
rest = " morning!"
full = first + rest
print(full)

line = "-" * 20
print(line)
blocks.caption: + joins strings together; * repeats a string.

blocks.title: You Cannot + a String and a Number
blocks.md: Python does not automatically convert numbers to text. `"Score: " + 10` will raise a `TypeError`. You must convert the number first: `"Score: " + str(10)`. This is a very common beginner mistake!

blocks.code:
score = 42
message = "Your score is: " + str(score)
print(message)
blocks.caption: Use str() to convert a number before concatenating it with a string.

blocks.md: `len()` is a built-in Python function that tells you how many characters are in a string — spaces and punctuation included. Think of it as counting every bead on that necklace.

blocks.caption: len() counts every character, including spaces and punctuation.

blocks.md: Here is something important: once a string is created, **you cannot change any individual character inside it**. This property is called **immutability** — strings are locked in place. If you want a different string, you create a new one.

blocks.code:
name = "Alice"
# This will cause an error:
# name[0] = "B"   <-- TypeError!

# The right way: build a new string
name = "B" + name[1:]
print(name)
blocks.caption: You cannot modify a string in place. Instead, build and assign a new string.

blocks.title: Why Are Strings Immutable?
blocks.md: Immutability makes strings **safe and predictable**. When you pass a string to a function, you know the original cannot be secretly changed. Python can also optimize memory by reusing identical strings behind the scenes.

blocks.title: Reassigning is Fine
blocks.md: You can always **replace** the whole string by assigning a new value to the variable: `name = "Bob"`. That does not mutate the old string — it just points the variable at a brand-new one.

blocks.question:
What will the following code print?

```python
word = "hi"
result = word * 3 + "!"
print(result)
```
blocks.explanation: `word * 3` repeats the string "hi" three times, giving `"hihihi"`. Then `+ "!"` concatenates the exclamation mark, producing `"hihihi!"`.

keyTakeaways: Strings can be created with single quotes, double quotes, or triple quotes — all work the same way.
Escape sequences like `\n` (newline) and `\t` (tab) let you embed special characters inside a string.
Use `+` to join strings and `*` to repeat them; always convert numbers with `str()` before concatenating.
`len()` counts every character in a string, including spaces and punctuation.
Strings are immutable — you cannot change a character in place; instead, build a new string.

exercise.prompt: Create a variable called `full_name` by concatenating a first name and a last name with a space in between. Then print a message that reads: "Hello, [full_name]! Your name has [N] characters." — where N is the length of the full name (not counting the greeting itself).
exercise.starterCode:
first = "Ada"
last = "Lovelace"

# Combine first and last with a space
full_name = 

# Print the greeting
exercise.hint: Use + to join the strings (don't forget the space!), then use len(full_name) to get the length, and str() to convert it before concatenating into the message.
exercise.solution:
first = "Ada"
last = "Lovelace"

full_name = first + " " + last

print("Hello, " + full_name + "! Your name has " + str(len(full_name)) + " characters.")
